patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 2/3] btrfs: fix incorrect buffered IO fallback for append direct writes
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a potential issue in Btrfs where buffered IO fallback for append direct writes could lead to incorrect i_size updates, causing readers to block on an out-of-evaluation (OE) error and potentially return zeroes. The problem arises from the fact that the invalid i_size is only fixed up within the dio thread holding the inode lock, but released before the extent lock is acquired. Boris Burkov suggests this could lead to a reader observing the intermediate too-big i_size before it's corrected. The patch aims to clarify and fix this potential issue.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the current patch does not address a potential race condition between buffered reads and direct writes, where a buffered reader could observe an intermediate too-big i_size before it is fixed, leading to incorrect behavior.

Reviewer noted that all i_size_write() calls are under the extent lock, and suggested keeping EXTENT_DIO_LOCKED hold until iomap_end() to properly update isize
review_comments.raw_body:
Buffered read doesn't take inode lock at all, thus inode lock doesn't 
seem enough.

Although I'm not sure if it's a good idea to mix buffered read with 
direct IO in the real world.

---

I just checked all i_size_write() calls inside btrfs, it looks like they 
are all under extent lock.

So even just for the sake of consistency we should already follow the 
existing requirement.


I'll try to explore some methods to properly update the isize.

My current idea is to keep the EXTENT_DIO_LOCKED hold until iomap_end(), 
so that we can properly update the isize inside iomap_end().
But I'm not sure if it's safe or we may have some other hidden pitfalls.

Thanks,
Qu

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch proposes adding a contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock, and the discussion revolves around implementing it using static_call or static_branch.

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the implementation approach, suggesting a custom callback for tracepoint enable/disable and noting potential issues with tail-calling or creating separate unlock functions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Jun 03, 2026 at 02:17:27PM +0000, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:

> > Something a little like so, which is completely untested, except to
> > build kernel/locking/spinlock.o (with clang-23).
> 
> Thanks a lot for taking a look, Peter.
> 
> I like the static_call idea. It's truly zero cost on x86 (and, as you
> note, even a byte smaller). The one caveat is that it relies on
> HAVE_STATIC_CALL_INLINE to stay free. 
> 
> So my plan would be: static_call where HAVE_STATIC_CALL_INLINE is
> available (x86), and a static branch fallback elsewhere, gated behind a
> default-off config so it imposes nothing on arches/kernels that don't
> opt in. I'm mostly interested in x86, but would like arm64 to work too,
> which would use the fallback.

(i386 doesn't have STATIC_CALL_INLINE, but nobody cares about the
performance on that target, so anything goes really ;-)

> 
> Concretely:
> 
> 1. Split the sleepable-lock patches out and send them separately.
>    They're independent of the static call work and look far less
>    controversial.
> 
> 2. Convert the paravirt spinlock unlock to a static_call, as the
>    foundation for the unlock tracepoint. I'm happy to take a stab at it.
>    Let me know if you'd rather do it yourself.

Yeah, I think that patch as-is *should* work, but like said, I haven't
even tried it, so it could be terribly broken :-)

> 3. Build the unlock tracepoint on top: static_call where it's cheap,
>    config-gated static_branch fallback where it isn't.

Right, so I think we need some sort of custom callback for tracepoint
enable/disable. Its been a minute since I dug through the tracepoint
code, but I don't think it provides that with a convenient wrapper, but
it should be doable.

One thing to note is that when you set the tracepoint unlock function,
it should either tail-call into the original function, or you have to
create two unlock_trace functions, one for native and one for paravirt
and pick the right one.

> Does this plan sound reasonable to you?

Yeah, should work.

> > Also, I think someone should go do some performance runs with
> > ARCH_INLINE_SPIN_* set for x86 just like for s390.
> 
> That's a good point, I'll run benchmarks and report back with the
> results.

Thanks!

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Proposed a plan to implement the contended_release tracepoint using static_call or static_branch, and offered to run benchmarks to evaluate performance.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch enables access to private memory nodes through the /dev/xxx pattern, which is a simpler approach than implementing mbind support. The author believes that full mbind support requires more development time and introduces complex interactions with page allocation, migration, cgroups/cpusets, and UAPI. The patch focuses on enabling single-node bind for private nodes, which is considered the most feasible mempolicy to apply.

Reviewer noted that controlling fallbacks for N_MEMORY_PRIVATE in CDM is similar to the current patch, but highlighted a design decision that needs consideration.

Reviewer Balbir Singh noted that reclaim support is necessary for private memory, allowing it to be overcommitted like regular memory, and suggested discussing this further.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/9] nfsd: fixes for locally-triggerable bugs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/9] nfsd: fixes for locally-triggerable bugs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/9] nfsd: defer vfree of compound ops to fix rpc_status UAF
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the NFSv4 server by deferring the freeing of compound operation buffers until after an RCU grace period. The rpc_status netlink dumpit walks in-flight requests and reads opnums from these buffers, but the buffers are freed synchronously elsewhere in the code. By using kvfree_rcu_mightsleep() to defer the free, the patch ensures that the dumpit can safely read the buffers without accessing freed memory.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/9] nfsd: hold rcu across localio cmpxchg retry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server (nfsd) where a freed nfsd_file object can be reused, leading to dereferencing of freed memory. The fix involves holding RCU (Read-Copy Update) lock across a cmpxchg retry block in the nfsd_open_local_fh function, ensuring that the slab page backing the nfsd_file is not recycled until the RCU grace period elapses. This prevents a non-zero word at the nf_ref offset of the recycled object from making the refcount bump appear to succeed, and subsequently dereferencing new->nf_net and new->nf_file out of freed memory.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/9] nfs/localio: fix ref leak on nfs_uuid_add_file failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a reference leak in the NFS/localio code by releasing caller-owned file and net references when nfs_uuid_add_file() fails due to racing with nfs_uuid_put(). The fix involves using an RCU pointer to safely drop these references, allowing the slot pair and underlying struct file, dentry, and inode to be properly cleaned up. This patch addresses a previously fixed issue that was reintroduced in a later commit.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/9] nfsd: guard nfsd_serv deref in nfsd_file_net_dispose
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd service thread loop where it attempts to wake up a NULL svc_serv pointer, leading to a dereference error. The fix involves loading the nn->nfsd_serv pointer into a local variable and only calling svc_wake_up() when it is non-NULL. This matches the pattern used in the sibling helper function nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed(). The patch addresses an issue introduced by a previous commit that left file closing work to a work queue, which was fixed with this change.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/9] nfsd: widen nfsd_genl_rqstp address fields to sockaddr_storage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue in the nfsd kernel module where IPv6 addresses were being truncated when sent to userspace via netlink attributes. The problem occurred because the address fields in the struct nfsd_genl_rqstp were declared as plain 'struct sockaddr' (16 bytes), which is not large enough to hold an IPv6 address. To fix this, the patch widens these fields to 'struct sockaddr_storage', which can hold larger addresses. Additionally, it ensures that the full address is captured in memcpy calls and initializes the genl_rqstp stack variable to prevent leaking uninitialized data. This patch resolves a bug introduced by a previous commit and fixes an issue that could be exploited by unprivileged processes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/9] nfsd: fix refcount leak in nfsd_file_lru_add on insertion failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a latent bug in the NFS server's file cache, where an incremented reference count is never released when inserting a file into the LRU fails. This causes a permanent inflation of the refcount, preventing eviction of the affected file cache entry for the lifetime of the nfsd instance. The fix involves adding a compensating refcount decrement on failure and changing the WARN_ON to WARN_ON_ONCE to prevent log flooding. Additionally, the patch returns early on failure to skip unnecessary calls to nfsd_file_schedule_laundrette().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/9] nfsd: fix fcache_disposal UAF by inlining dispose state into nfsd_net
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free (UAF) bug in the NFS daemon's file cache disposal mechanism by inlining the dispose state into the nfsd_net structure. The original code allocated a separate struct for the freeme queue, which was freed during per-net teardown, causing dangling pointers when accessed by other threads. By moving the spinlock and freeme list directly into the nfsd_net structure, the patch ensures that these fields have the same lifetime as the net namespace itself, preventing the UAF bug. The changes also simplify the code by removing alloc/free helpers and modifying the initialization and shutdown functions to work with the new inline fields.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 8/9] nfsd: hold net namespace reference for delayed-dispose nfsd_files
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a use-after-free issue in the NFS daemon by introducing a new flag, NFSD_FILE_NET_HELD, to track whether an nfsd_file holds a net reference. It takes a net-namespace reference in nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed() and releases it in nfsd_file_free(). The patch also updates nfsd_file_put_local() to release the net reference before dropping the file reference, ensuring that the returned pointer remains valid.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: The reviewer notes that multiple LLM (Linux Lock Manager) reviewers have expressed difficulties with this patch, but does not specify what those issues are.
review_comments.raw_body:
It seems that all of the LLM reviewers have difficulty with this patch.
This is a consolidated review of the issue from Claude and Codex:

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addresses a concern about the safety of holding a net namespace reference for delayed-dispose nfsd_files, explaining that the struct net is guaranteed to be live at the point where get_net() is called and that the placement of get_net() is correct. The author asserts that this is not a real bug but rather a false positive.
review_comments.raw_body:
I had claude review this and it says:

● This is the same concern I just addressed for the previous patch's Finding 3, restated as a
  critical bug. The answer is the same: this is a false positive.

  The reviewer's scenario requires:

  1. The global shrinker unhashes and isolates an nfsd_file onto a local dispose list
  2. The net namespace teardown completes and struct net is freed
  3. The shrinker resumes and calls get_net() on freed memory

  Step 2 cannot happen before step 3. Here's why:

  The net namespace struct net is reference-counted. It isn't freed when nfsd_net_exit() runs --
  nfsd_net_exit() is a pernet subsys callback that runs during net namespace teardown, but the
  struct net itself stays alive until its own refcount drops to zero. The struct net outlives all
  pernet exit callbacks.

  More importantly, nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed() is called from exactly three places:
  - nfsd_file_gc() -- the GC delayed work
  - nfsd_file_lru_scan() -- the shrinker
  - nfsd_file_close_inode() -- fsnotify

  All three isolate items and call nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed() in a single, synchronous
  function call. There's no point where a local dispose list sits around waiting while the net
  namespace is torn down. The isolation and the get_net() happen in the same execution context.

  The shrinker and GC worker are global and remain registered until nfsd_file_cache_shutdown()
  (global teardown). Per-net teardown via nfsd_file_cache_shutdown_net() does not unregister them.
  But the files they process were allocated while the net namespace was alive, and nf->nf_net was
  set at allocation time in nfsd_file_alloc(). The struct net cannot have a zero refcount while nfsd
  files referencing it still exist in the LRU -- the nfsd server itself holds net references
  through its operation.

  The get_net() here is not protecting against the current dereference being unsafe. It's ensuring
  that nf_net remains valid for the arbitrarily long time the file sits on the per-net dispose list
  waiting for an nfsd thread to close it. That's exactly the right pattern.

  Verdict: False positive. The get_net() placement is correct and the struct net is guaranteed to be
  live at this point.

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever raised two concerns: (1) in nfsd_file_put_local(), the patch removes a necessary ordering between file ref and net ref release, potentially causing a stale-net hazard; he suggested putting the file ref first to ensure proper ordering. (2) in nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed(), the get_net() placement is incorrect because it can lead to touching freed memory if a worker is preempted between isolating a file and calling dispose_list_delayed(); he proposed taking the reference at alloc time for GC-capable files only, as done in v1.
review_comments.raw_body:
That rebuttal answers a different finding. The three-step scenario it
refutes is the one sashiko raised against the get_net() placement in
nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed(). The review quoted above is about
the put order in nfsd_file_put_local() and does not involve struct
net's refcount at any step. Two new issues appear with 8/9:

1. Put order in nfsd_file_put_local()
  
The question is narrow: after an I/O completion thread executes
nfsd_net_put() -- dropping the last nfsd_net_ref and running
complete(&nn->nfsd_net_free_done) -- what prevents nfsd_shutdown_net()
from continuing through percpu_ref_exit() and nfsd_shutdown_generic()
into nfsd_file_cache_shutdown() before that same thread executes the
nfsd_file_put() on the next line?

In the current code the answer is the ref itself: the caller holds it
across nfsd_file_put(), and percpu_ref runs the release callback only
after every ref drops, so global teardown strictly follows the file
free and the rcu_barrier() in nfsd_file_cache_shutdown() flushes the
call_rcu() that nfsd_file_free() queued. The patch removes that
ordering and installs nothing in its place.

The nfs_uuid handshake does not cover this path: each pgio holds its
own nfsd_file + nfsd_net ref pair (fs/nfs/pagelist.c, stored in
iocb->localio), released at I/O completion through
nfsd_file_put_local(), and an iocb is not on nfs_uuid->files. The
purge has already unhashed the file but cannot free it
(nfsd_file_cond_queue() sees the elevated refcount), so the
completion thread's put is the final one and its nfsd_file_free()
races kmem_cache_destroy(nfsd_file_slab).

The stale-net hazard the commit message cites cannot occur on this
path: NFSD_FILE_NET_HELD is set only via refcount_dec_if_one() in
nfsd_file_lru_cb(), i.e. at refcount == 1, and a file with an
outstanding LOCALIO reference has refcount >= 2. So the fix is to
keep the void API but put the file ref first, then the net ref.

2. get_net() placement in nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed()

The rebuttal's struct-net argument does not hold for the files in
question: they are no longer on the LRU (nfsd_file_lru_cb() unhashed
and isolated them onto the worker's private list), and an nfsd_file
holds no net reference -- that absence is what this patch is fixing.

The commit message itself states the premise: the purge cannot see
these files and nf_net can dangle. With a second nfsd-serving netns
keeping nfsd_users > 0, nothing quiesces the shrinker or the
laundrette during per-net teardown (cancel_delayed_work_sync() and
shrinker_free() run only in global shutdown).

A worker preempted between isolating a file and calling
dispose_list_delayed() can resume after cleanup_net() has freed the
namespace, at which point get_net(nf->nf_net), net_generic(), and
the fcache_dispose_lock all touch freed memory. The get_net() sits
at the consuming end of the window it is meant to close. v1 took
the reference in nfsd_file_alloc(), the one place guaranteed to run
while the net is alive; the v2 plan of taking it at alloc time for
GC-capable files only would address Al's cacheline concern without
reopening the window.


As a process note, I'll note that I haven't found any non-pre-
existing issues with the other patches on this series.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about potential use-after-free when nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed() calls net_generic(), agreed to restructure the patch in v2 and will resend it separately.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, I'm convinced, and the fix looks fairly straightforward.

If you're ok with merging the rest of the set, I'll plan to resend this
one separately.

Thanks,
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 9/9] nfsd: unify cleanups in nfsd_cross_mnt() exits
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the nfsd_cross_mnt() function to move all path_put() and exp_put() calls to a single location after the codepaths join, ensuring consistent ordering between these operations. This change simplifies the cleanup process in this critical function, reducing complexity and improving maintainability.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses several bugs in the NFS daemon (nfsd) that can be triggered by a local user, including some that require administrative access. The patches simplify file cache disposal and fix issues related to reference counting, memory management, and cleanup operations. They also unify cleanups in nfsd_cross_mnt() exits and address potential use-after-free bugs. The changes aim to improve the stability and security of the NFS daemon by fixing these locally-triggerable bugs.

(2) in the dispose_list_delayed() function, get_net(nf->nf_net) touches freed memory if a worker is preempted between isolating a file and calling dispose_list_delayed(), because cleanup_net() has already freed the namespace.
review_comments.raw_body:
That rebuttal answers a different finding. The three-step scenario it
refutes is the one sashiko raised against the get_net() placement in
nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed(). The review quoted above is about
the put order in nfsd_file_put_local() and does not involve struct
net's refcount at any step. Two new issues appear with 8/9:

1. Put order in nfsd_file_put_local()
  
The question is narrow: after an I/O completion thread executes
nfsd_net_put() -- dropping the last nfsd_net_ref and running
complete(&nn->nfsd_net_free_done) -- what prevents nfsd_shutdown_net()
from continuing through percpu_ref_exit() and nfsd_shutdown_generic()
into nfsd_file_cache_shutdown() before that same thread executes the
nfsd_file_put() on the next line?

In the current code the answer is the ref itself: the caller holds it
across nfsd_file_put(), and percpu_ref runs the release callback only
after every ref drops, so global teardown strictly follows the file
free and the rcu_barrier() in nfsd_file_cache_shutdown() flushes the
call_rcu() that nfsd_file_free() queued. The patch removes that
ordering and installs nothing in its place.

The nfs_uuid handshake does not cover this path: each pgio holds its
own nfsd_file + nfsd_net ref pair (fs/nfs/pagelist.c, stored in
iocb->localio), released at I/O completion through
nfsd_file_put_local(), and an iocb is not on nfs_uuid->files. The
purge has already unhashed the file but cannot free it
(nfsd_file_cond_queue() sees the elevated refcount), so the
completion thread's put is the final one and its nfsd_file_free()
races kmem_cache_destroy(nfsd_file_slab).

The stale-net hazard the commit message cites cannot occur on this
path: NFSD_FILE_NET_HELD is set only via refcount_dec_if_one() in
nfsd_file_lru_cb(), i.e. at refcount == 1, and a file with an
outstanding LOCALIO reference has refcount >= 2. So the fix is to
keep the void API but put the file ref first, then the net ref.

2. get_net() placement in nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed()

The rebuttal's struct-net argument does not hold for the files in
question: they are no longer on the LRU (nfsd_file_lru_cb() unhashed
and isolated them onto the worker's private list), and an nfsd_file
holds no net reference -- that absence is what this patch is fixing.

The commit message itself states the premise: the purge cannot see
these files and nf_net can dangle. With a second nfsd-serving netns
keeping nfsd_users > 0, nothing quiesces the shrinker or the
laundrette during per-net teardown (cancel_delayed_work_sync() and
shrinker_free() run only in global shutdown).

A worker preempted between isolating a file and calling
dispose_list_delayed() can resume after cleanup_net() has freed the
namespace, at which point get_net(nf->nf_net), net_generic(), and
the fcache_dispose_lock all touch freed memory. The get_net() sits
at the consuming end of the window it is meant to close. v1 took
the reference in nfsd_file_alloc(), the one place guaranteed to run
while the net is alive; the v2 plan of taking it at alloc time for
GC-capable files only would address Al's cacheline concern without
reopening the window.


As a process note, I'll note that I haven't found any non-pre-
existing issues with the other patches on this series.


-- 
Chuck Lever

Re: [PATCH v2 8/9] nfsd: hold net namespace reference for delayed-dispose nfsd_files
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a false positive concern about a potential bug in the nfsd code. The issue was that the global shrinker unhashes and isolates an nfsd_file onto a local dispose list, but then the net namespace teardown completes and struct net is freed before the shrinker resumes and calls get_net() on freed memory. However, the reviewer's analysis shows that this scenario cannot happen because the struct net outlives all pernet exit callbacks, including nfsd_net_exit(), and the isolation and get_net() happen in the same execution context. The patch does not change any code, but rather confirms that the existing placement of get_net() is correct and ensures that nf_net remains valid for an arbitrarily long time while the file sits on the per-net dispose list waiting for an nfsd thread to close it.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a long-standing bug in the list_lru code, where the shrinker bit is set on the wrong cgroup when there's a race between list_lru_add and cgroup deletion. The fix involves returning the 'effective' memcg from the locking function and having the caller use that.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by removing redundant code and unifying parameter names to improve readability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the memory management in the Linux kernel by flattening the alloc_anon_folio() retry loop, making it easier to read and maintain. The current implementation uses a top-level if statement that buries the success path four levels deep, which is addressed by this patch.

review_comments.author: Dev Jain
review_comments.summary: Provided a Reviewed-by tag without raising any specific technical concerns or suggestions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change addresses a suboptimal handling of cgroups in the deferred split queue, where large pages on other nodes were being split unnecessarily. The new approach streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers already. The patch also adds calls to create node heads for the cgroup at once when splittable pages are created, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a folio is split across multiple nodes, and requested that the list_lru per-memcg heads be instantiated on demand when the first object of interest is allocated for a cgroup.

Reviewer SeongJae Park reported that the patch causes a build failure in UM mode due to an implicit function declaration error, specifically for the folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() function when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is unset. They suggested defining the function for this case and provided a temporal fix as evidence.

Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch is missing a definition for folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE is unset, causing an implicit function declaration error in UM mode kunit tests.

The reviewer pointed out that the patch does not filter shmem swap-in operations from using folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(), which allocates memcg list_lru metadata for deferred_split_lru, but only queues anon large folios. shmem_get_folio_gfp()

only allocates the memcg list_lru metadata for deferred_split_lru:

int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio)

shmem swapin doesn't buy us anything :)

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of the current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit() where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg instead of the ancestor that received the item. Other changes include adapting to the new lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature, propagating folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure, and fixing a use-after-free bug in deferred_split_isolate(). The patches have been rebased onto the latest mm-unstable tree.

review_comments.author: Lance Yang
review_comments.summary: Lance Yang noted that the new list_lru deferred split queue may lose reclaim in the cgroup.memory=nokmem case, and requested further investigation.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Johannes,

On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:07PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>This is version 5 of switching the THP shrinker to list_lru.
>
>Core of the new version is the list_lru/set_shrinker_bit fix up front,
>which minimally affects later patches; and a rebase onto the latest
>mm-unstable - replaced alloc_swap_folio() with __swap_cache_alloc().
>
>The changes seemed small enough that *I chose to keep the review tags
>from v4*. Please shout if you object to this!
>
>Changes in v5:
>- patch 1 is a new fix for a very old, pre-existing set_shrinker_bit()
>  problem in list_lru, where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg
>  instead of the ancestor that actually received the item. Pointed out
>  by Usama Arif and Sashiko; fix it first to make it minimally
>  backportable and so the conversion is safe.
>- patches 6 and 9 adapt to that fix's new memcg-by-reference
>  lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature
>- collapse_huge_page(): propagate folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure
>  as SCAN_ALLOC_HUGE_PAGE_FAIL instead of leaking SCAN_SUCCEED and
>  falsely reporting a successful MADV_COLLAPSE (Usama Arif, Sashiko)
>- deferred_split_isolate(): fix a UAF by reading folio state before
>  list_lru_isolate(); once removed, a racing folio_put() frees the
>  folio via the lockless list_empty() check while we still touch its
>  flags and stats (Sashiko)
>- rebased to mm-unstable of 2026-05-27, which simplifies the flatten
>  prep patch (now anon-only, as alloc_swap_folio() was folded into the
>  new __swap_cache_alloc()) and moves the swap-side
>  folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() hook into __swap_cache_alloc(). Kairui,
>  I would appreciate an eyeball on that.
>
>Changes in v4:
>- guard folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() with mem_cgroup_disabled() to fix
>  NULL deref in __memcg_list_lru_alloc() when booting with
>  cgroup_disable=memory (e.g., kdump capture kernel) -- reported and
>  tested by Mikhail Zaslonko on s390 and x86
>- flatten if (folio) branches in alloc_swap_folio() and alloc_anon_folio()
>  in a prep patch so the list_lru allocation additions are a clean minimal
>  diff (Lorenzo)
>- folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() moved out of alloc_charge_folio() into the
>  anon-only collapse_huge_page() path; collapse_file() shares that helper
>  but its pages don't go on the THP shrinker queue (David)
>- guard folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() with order > 1; mTHPs below order-2
>  can't be queued on the deferred split list (David)
>- make deferred_split_lru static, hide behind folio_memcg_alloc_deferred()
>  wrapper with GFP_KERNEL (Lorenzo)
>- rename l -> lru throughout huge_memory.c (Lorenzo)
>- kdoc for folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() (Lorenzo)
>- list_lru_lock_irq()/unlock_irq()/add_irq() irq-disabling variants;
>  use list_lru_add_irq() in deferred_split_scan() (Lorenzo)
>- reorder shrinker_free() before list_lru_destroy() (Lorenzo)
>
>Changes in v3:
>- dedicated lockdep_key for irqsafe deferred_split_lru.lock (syzbot)
>- conditional list_lru ops in __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped() (syzbot)
>- annotate runs of inscrutable false, NULL, false function arguments (David)
>- rename to folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() (David)
>
>Changes in v2:
>- explicit rcu_read_lock() in __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped() (Usama)
>- split out list_lru prep bits (Dave)
>
>The open-coded deferred split queue has issues. It's not NUMA-aware
>(when cgroup is enabled), and it's more complicated in the callsites
>interacting with it. Switching to list_lru fixes the NUMA problem and
>streamlines things. It also simplifies planned shrinker work.
>
>Patch 1 fixes a pre-existing list_lru bug where the shrinker bit is
>set on the caller's memcg rather than the ancestor whose sublist the
>item actually lands on after a walk-up. Standalone, backportable; the
>rest of the series depends on it.
>
>Patches 2-5 are cleanups and small refactors in list_lru code. They're
>basically independent, but make the THP shrinker conversion easier.
>
>Patch 6 extends the list_lru API to allow the caller to control the
>locking scope. The THP shrinker has private state it needs to keep
>synchronized with the LRU state.
>
>Patch 7 extends the list_lru API with a convenience helper to do
>list_lru head allocation (memcg_list_lru_alloc) when coming from a
>folio. Anon THPs are instantiated in several places, and with the
>folio reparenting patches pending, folio_memcg() access is now a more
>delicate dance. This avoids having to replicate that dance everywhere.
>
>Patch 8 flattens the alloc_anon_folio() retry loop so the next patch's
>list_lru hook lands as a clean addition rather than nested deep inside
>an if (folio) block.
>
>Patch 9 finally switches the deferred_split_queue to list_lru.

As the changelog above says, the old queue is per-memcg only, rather
than per-memcg-per-node. So reclaim on one node can still walk the whole
memcg queue and split underused THPs from other nodes in the same memcg.

But I think the new one can lose reclaim in the cgroup.memory=nokmem
case ...

With nokmem, the deferred shrinker can still run from memcg reclaim,
because it is SHRINKER_NONSLAB. But the list_lru is no longer per-memcg:

__list_lru_init() clears memcg_aware,

	if (mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
		memcg_aware = false;

so list_lru_from_memcg_idx() falls back to the shared node list:

static inline struct list_lru_one *
list_lru_from_memcg_idx(struct list_lru *lru, int nid, int idx)
{
	if (list_lru_memcg_aware(lru) && idx >= 0) {
[...]
	}
	return &lru->node[nid].lru;
}

That makes the shrinker bit unreliable. __list_lru_add() still sets the
bit on the memcg passed in, but only when the list goes from empty to
non-empty:

bool __list_lru_add(struct list_lru *lru, struct list_lru_one *l,
		    struct list_head *item, int nid,
		    struct mem_cgroup *memcg)
{
	if (list_empty(item)) {
[...]
		if (!l->nr_items++)
			set_shrinker_bit(memcg, nid, lru_shrinker_id(lru));
[...]
		return true;
	}
	return false;
}

If memcg A adds the first folio, A gets the bit. If memcg B later adds a
folio to the same shared list, B does not get a bit, because the list
was already non-empty.

So in the A-first/B-later case, reclaim from B may not call the deferred
shrinker at all. The shared list is scanned from memcg reclaim only if
reclaim runs from the memcg that has the bit, such as A here, or from
global reclaim :)

Anyway, only after the shared list is emptied does the next memcg to add
a folio get to be the one with the bit, IIUC :)

Hopefully I didn't miss somthing important ...

Cheers, Lance

---

Shouldn't this be limited to anon swapin?

e.g. vmf && vma_is_anonymous(vmf->vma)

>+	if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {

__swap_cache_alloc() is also used by shmem direct swapin, so shmem can
get here too when handling a large swap entry:

shmem_get_folio_gfp()
  shmem_swapin_folio()
    shmem_swap_alloc_folio()
      swapin_sync()
        swap_cache_alloc_folio()
          __swap_cache_alloc()
            folio_memcg_alloc_deferred()

@Baolin please correct me if I got it wrong :)

folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() itself doesn't filter shmem out either; it
only allocates the memcg list_lru metadata for deferred_split_lru:

int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio)
{
	if (mem_cgroup_disabled())
		return 0;
	return folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(folio, &deferred_split_lru, GFP_KERNEL);
}

Since deferred_split_lru only queues anon large folios, doing this for
shmem swapin doesn't buy us anything :)

Maybe I'm missing something, just wanted to raise it.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 0/9] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru, which is tied to kmem component of memcg. This change has negligible impact on memcg semantics but would break isolation if kmem is disabled.
